_description: JP Miller voice profile — extracted from 5 published blog posts. Style rules only, no content enters prompts at runtime.
_status: CALIBRATED from reference/samples/ (Barcelona JRPG, AI Cameras Circuits, GPU Comparison, Rosetta Initiative, Child Mind Institute)
_extraction_method: Pattern analysis across 5 published blogs totaling ~38,700 characters

sentence_rhythm.short_punch: Use 3-8 word declarative sentences to punctuate key moments or section transitions. Examples from samples: 'The delays killed any sense of creative momentum.' 'Rather than fold, he pivoted.' One per paragraph maximum.
sentence_rhythm.medium_flow: Default sentence length: 12-20 words. Clear subject-verb-object, one qualifier allowed. This is the workhorse sentence.
sentence_rhythm.long_build: Occasional 22-30 word sentences to layer context or build technical explanation. Never two in a row. Use when stacking specific details that earn the length.
sentence_rhythm.variation_rule: Never write 3+ sentences of similar length consecutively. The natural pattern in samples: medium-medium-short, or medium-long-short. Short sentences earn their impact by following longer ones.

paragraph_cadence.length_variation: Mix 1-sentence, 2-sentence, and 3-4 sentence paragraphs. Samples average 2.8 sentences per paragraph. Never exceed 5 sentences.
paragraph_cadence.section_endings: End sections with a short, declarative statement. Period, not question mark. Forward-leaning when possible — hint at what comes next.
paragraph_cadence.opening_variety: Vary paragraph openings: proper noun, action verb, specific detail, temporal marker, quote lead-in. Never start 2 consecutive paragraphs the same way. Samples show strong preference for opening with the subject's name or a concrete action.
paragraph_cadence.single_sentence_paragraphs: Used sparingly for emphasis or transition. Effective after a longer paragraph to create breathing room. Samples use about 1 per 5-6 paragraphs.

word_choice.prefer_concrete: Choose specific verbs: 'froze,' 'collapsed,' 'stalled,' 'registered,' 'deployed' over generic alternatives. Samples favor verbs that imply physical action even in technical contexts.
word_choice.favor_periods: Prefer periods over semicolons. Samples use semicolons rarely (roughly 1 per 800 words). Prefer two short sentences over one compound sentence.
word_choice.minimize_qualifiers: Cut 'very,' 'really,' 'quite,' 'somewhat,' 'significant.' Samples replace qualifiers with specifics: not 'significantly faster' but '45 minutes instead of two and a half hours.'
word_choice.active_specificity: Name the tool, the person, the place, the number. 'Maya crashed during the render' not 'the software had issues.' '120,000 players' not 'a large user base.'
word_choice.numbers_as_drama: Use specific numbers to create tension or show transformation: '€50,000 goal in two hours,' 'two and a half hours to 45 minutes,' 'one in five children.' Numbers replace adjectives.

structural_patterns.narrative_entry: Open with a named person and what they do. Ground the reader in a specific human before introducing the problem or technology. Never open with an industry generalization. Samples consistently open with: '[Name] [does/is/has] [specific thing].'
structural_patterns.problem_before_tech: Introduce the constraint, frustration, or challenge before the technology that addresses it. The human struggle earns the reader's investment; the tech payoff lands harder after tension. Every sample follows this pattern.
structural_patterns.quote_integration: Set up quotes with narrative context that makes the quote land. The sentence before a quote should establish the situation; the quote then delivers the voice. Never use 'said:' pattern. Samples favor '[Context sentence.] "Quote," [attribution with additional context].'
structural_patterns.tension_before_resolution: Always present the constraint or problem BEFORE the achievement. Show what was broken, slow, or impossible first. The transformation earns meaning from contrast.
structural_patterns.closing_style: End with forward motion: what happens next, what this enables, where the subject is headed. Final paragraphs in samples introduce new information (a future release, a broader implication) rather than summarizing. CTA is woven into the narrative resolution, never bolted on.
structural_patterns.subheading_usage: Subheadings mark thesis progression, not cosmetic breaks. Samples average 3-4 subheadings per blog. Each marks a genuine shift: from backstory to problem, problem to resolution, resolution to future.

anti_patterns.no_throat_clearing: Cut opening phrases: 'When it comes to,' 'In the world of,' 'It's no secret that,' 'In today's landscape.' Start with the subject or action.
anti_patterns.no_false_balance: Don't hedge with 'While X is true, Y is also important.' Samples commit to a direction. When comparison is needed (GPU article), let data speak instead of hedging language.
anti_patterns.no_list_exhaustion: Don't enumerate every feature or benefit. Pick the 2-3 most vivid examples and let them represent the whole. Samples rarely list more than 3 items in sequence.
anti_patterns.no_echo_conclusions: The conclusion must add something new — a future milestone, a broader implication, a lingering question. Never restate the introduction or summarize the body.
anti_patterns.no_passive_attribution: Don't write 'It was noted that' or 'It has been observed.' Name who did what. Samples consistently use active voice with named subjects.
anti_patterns.no_tech_worship: Technology is context, not the headline. The human's story comes first. Dell and NVIDIA appear as tools the subject uses, not as the protagonist. Samples mention brand names 2-4 times in the body, always in service of the narrative.
